dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 8 responding for reported overdose or possible drug ingestion at --Address Redacted-- in Richmond.
Hello, my name is Jessica and I'm an EMT. Can you help us by sharing what happened?
She became very confused this morning and difficult to reach after the police arrived. It's possible she took heroin.
Thank you for that. May we ask you a few questions to better understand what's going on?
Can you open your eyes for me, ma'am?
Eye opens slowly and partially.
What is your name?
Lisa.
Lisa, can you squeeze my fingers for me?
...Slight movement of fingers.
She was talking and alert when the police first arrived around 10:00.
She was fully awake then.
When the officer first made contact with her, she was oriented and could speak coherently. A GCS of 15 was noted at that time.
Lisa, can we quickly assess your airway to make sure you're breathing comfortably?
Eye opens slightly. Nods once.
Your airway appears clear and you're maintaining it well.
Let me check your breathing. Can you stay still for a few moments?
Respirations are slow, 10 per minute, but regular.
No abnormal breath sounds heard.
May I feel your radial pulse to assess your heart rate?
Pulse is 68, strong and regular. Skin color is normal and warm.
Pulse is 68, strong and regular.
Respirations are at 10.
Blood pressure is 137 over 87.
Blood glucose is 143 and stable.
SpO2 is 100%.
EKG shows sinus rhythm.
Lisa, can you tell me what symptoms you've been experiencing?
I've had a headache all morning.
Do you have any medical history we should be aware of?
I've had seizures before, but not in a long time.
Are you currently taking any medications?
No, I haven't taken anything for a while now.
What happened before the police arrived this morning?
She told me she injected heroin this morning.
And how old are you, Lisa?
When did the headache start this morning?
It began quite early this morning, not too long after shooting up.
How would you describe the pain?
It's about a 4 out of 10.
Lisa, can we shine a light in your eyes to check your pupils?
She opens her eyes slightly.
Pupils are 3 millimeters and equally reactive.
Are you able to stand at all?
She is standing, although slowly, and with assistance.
We are going to place you on a cardiac monitor for more detailed rhythm analysis.
4-lead monitoring is now on. Current rhythm is sinus.
Pulse is currently 60.
Respirations have increased to 14 per minute.
Blood pressure is now 134/92.
Blood glucose is now at 0-our device may not be reading correctly.
SpO2 remains steady at 100%.
EKG now indicates a first-degree AV block with sinus bradycardia.
Partner, I've completed the 12-lead ECG. For a 26-year-old, this is abnormal.
Based on the altered mental status, ECG findings, and current stability, we should transport to the nearest appropriate facility per protocol.
I am calling VCU Health Systems to confirm that we are transporting a 26-year-old female with altered mental status and ECG abnormalities.
Lisa, can you assist us by standing so we can help you onto the stretcher?
She is standing slowly, and we are pivoting her to the stretcher with assistance.
She is now secured on the stretcher in a left lateral position with 5-point restraints.
She is being loaded onto the ambulance via the Ferno stretcher.
She remains stable, and the hospital was notified enroute.
The ED nurse has been briefed on the patient's condition, and all relevant treatments have been documented.
She was found alert and oriented upon initial contact by the officer with a GCS of 15 and then became A&O x4 with a GCS of 13 by the time we arrived.
Care has been fully transferred to the ED nursing staff, and all findings were conveyed.
Due to legal constraints, the patient was not able to sign for herself. The arresting officer authorized care on her behalf.
All field treatments were consistent with medical protocol for altered mental status (Protocol 3-15).
The initial GCS was 13, and she is aware of her name and the current date, though demonstrates difficulty with orientation to time and place-awake but not fully with us (awake in four axes-AO x4).
There were no changes in her condition during transport, and vitals remained steady throughout.
Both 4- and 12-lead ECG procedures were performed according to standing orders.
Blood glucose dropped to 0, but since the patient remained stable at the time, no further action was taken at the scene.
Transport to VCU Health Systems was initiated following on-scene assessment and all agreed-upon treatments.
No seizures were observed or administered keppra, and the IV was refused by the patient.
All documentation and procedures were completed in line with field orders and legal standards.
The patient was handled from the scene with the assistance of a RPD officer and transported safely to the ED without incident.
Initial and subsequent vitals, as well as all medical assessments, were tracked and documented in a timely manner.
An overview of the patient's condition was provided both enroute and at ED, as reported by the officer upon initial contact.
The patient was moved using a pull sheet method from the ambulance to the ED bed by the EMS team.
No additional medications were administered and no significant changes in mental status or vitals occurred during transport.
All field impressions and observations remained consistent with altered mental status and possible drug ingestion.
No additional interventions or pain reassessments were needed enroute, and all necessary patient history was collected at the scene.
No protocol deviations occurred. All findings were confirmed and documented.
Transport time followed facility guidelines, and all necessary procedures were concluded as required.
A police officer ridealong was present for the duration of transport and during ED handoff.
All field procedures were reviewed and confirmed at arrival at the ED, with no required changes.
No observable pain reassessment was performed enroute as the patient remained stable and coherent.
No additional history was needed and all necessary care was provided.
All EMS procedures, documentation, and care were in compliance with local and state standards.
All observations are consistent with the clinical impression of altered mental status and possible poisoning.
Conclusion is that the patient remained stable throughout and was appropriately transported within protocol.
She was initially alert and orientated, became AO x4, and was recognized to be under police custody.
All field decisions were made in full consideration of custody status, including documentation and patient refusal of IV.
The GCS tracking (from 15 to 13) is fully documented, as is her acknowledgment of name and date but with a lack of proper orientation to place and time.
All interventions remained consistent with standing orders and legal guidelines.
No conflicts arose in delivery of care or decision-making, and all EMS actions followed protocol closely.
Patient remained stable and did not deviate in status or vitals from time of assessment through transport.
Notes from field report and verified by ridealong confirm no issues and a smooth transfer from field to ED.
Communication with ED was made enroute as mandated under protocol for altered mental status and abnormal ECG findings.
No further care beyond field instruction or additional medications was given.
The patient was transported to the appropriate facility, and proper documentation of all events and interventions was performed.
